---
title: "Your Paper Title"
author:
  - name: Your Name
    email: you@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Your Department, Your University
        address: Some Address, Country
        corresponding: true
  - name: Co-Author One
    email: one@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
  - name: Co-Author Two
    email: two@example.org
    affiliations:
      - name: Co-Author Department, Co-Author University
        address: Some Address, Country
date: today
date-format: "[This revision:] D MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  This template paper studies the relationship between an outcome
  variable and a focal regressor using a generic panel dataset.
  Replace this paragraph with your own abstract; aim for 150--250
  words covering the question, data, method, and main findings.

  \bigskip
  \noindent\textbf{Keywords:} keyword one, keyword two, keyword three

  \noindent\textbf{JEL Classification:} A00, B00, C00
keywords:
  - keyword one
  - keyword two
  - keyword three
thanks: |
  We thank seminar and conference participants for helpful comments.
  Replace this acknowledgments block with your own.
bibliography: references.bib

format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    documentclass: article
    classoption:
      - "12pt"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    linestretch: 2
    indent: true
    fontsize: 12pt
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: red
    citecolor: blue
    urlcolor: magenta
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: apalike
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
    subparagraph: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{amsthm}
        \usepackage{subcaption}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \usepackage{enumitem}
        \usepackage{multirow}
        \usepackage{longtable}
        \usepackage{tabularx}
        \usepackage{float}
        \usepackage{sectsty}

        \AtBeginEnvironment{tabular}{\onehalfspacing}

        % Inject the italic "Preliminary draft" line between the title
        % and the author block. We redefine \title so that whatever
        % the YAML supplies gets appended with a line break and the
        % italic subtitle line. Avoids the titling package, which
        % isn't always present in TinyTex installs.
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\title}[1]{%
          \gdef\@title{#1\\[0.3cm]%
            {\normalsize\itshape Preliminary draft --- please do not cite without permission}%
          }%
        }
        \makeatother

        % Italic subsection / subsubsection headings. sectsty alone
        % sometimes doesn't fully override the article-class default
        % \bfseries, so we also re-issue \@startsection directly with
        % \itshape in the font slot for belt-and-suspenders coverage.
        \subsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \subsubsectionfont{\large\itshape}
        \makeatletter
        \renewcommand{\subsection}{\@startsection{subsection}{2}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \renewcommand{\subsubsection}{\@startsection{subsubsection}{3}{\z@}%
          {-3.25ex \@plus -1ex \@minus -.2ex}{1.5ex \@plus .2ex}%
          {\normalfont\large\itshape}}
        \makeatother

        % Period-after-section numbering: headings read "1. Foo"
        % rather than "1 Foo".
        \makeatletter
        \def\@seccntformat#1{\@ifundefined{#1@cntformat}%
            {\csname the#1\endcsname\quad}%
            {\csname #1@cntformat\endcsname}%
        }
        \def\section@cntformat{\thesection.\quad}
        \def\subsection@cntformat{\thesubsection \quad}
        \makeatother

        \tolerance=1
        \emergencystretch=\maxdimen
        \hyphenpenalty=10000
        \hbadness=10000

        \renewenvironment{quote}
          {\normalsize\list{}{\rightmargin=1.5cm \leftmargin=1.5cm}%
           \item\relax}
          {\endlist}

        % Symmetric vspace before AND after equations.
        \let\oldequation=\equation
        \let\endoldequation=\endequation
        \renewenvironment{equation}{%
          \vspace{0.25cm}\begin{oldequation}}{\vspace{0.25cm}\end{oldequation}%
        }

        \theoremstyle{plain}
        \newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}[section]
        \newtheorem{proposition}[theorem]{Proposition}
        \newtheorem{lemma}[theorem]{Lemma}
        \theoremstyle{definition}
        \newtheorem{definition}[theorem]{Definition}
        \newtheorem{assumption}[theorem]{Assumption}
---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

This paper studies how a focal explanatory variable $x$ shapes an
outcome $y$ in a generic panel of units indexed by $i$ and time $t$.
The exercise is deliberately stylized: it serves as a working
example of the template's typography rather than a substantive
contribution.

The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section
@sec-theory sketches a stylized model. Section @sec-data describes
the (synthetic) data. Section @sec-method outlines the empirical
specification. Section @sec-results reports the main findings.
Sections @sec-additional and @sec-robust discuss extensions and
robustness. Section @sec-conc concludes.

# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

Consider an economy populated by a continuum of identical agents
indexed by $i$. Each agent chooses an action $x_{it}$ to maximize
an expected payoff that depends on a latent fundamental $\theta_t$
and an idiosyncratic shock $\varepsilon_{it}$. The equilibrium
mapping from fundamentals to outcomes is summarized by a function
$y_{it} = g(x_{it}, \theta_t) + \varepsilon_{it}$ whose properties
deliver the testable implications below.

## Testable Predictions {#sec-theory-predictions}

The model yields two predictions that we take to the data.

::: {.assumption}
The idiosyncratic shock $\varepsilon_{it}$ is mean-zero and
independent of the focal regressor $x_{it}$ conditional on the
unit fixed effect $\alpha_i$.
:::

::: {.proposition}
Under the assumption above, the marginal effect $\partial y / \partial x$
is identified from the within-unit variation in $x_{it}$.
:::

# Data {#sec-data}

We illustrate the empirical workflow on a synthetic panel of
1{,}000 units observed over 20 periods, generated to mimic the
kind of firm-level data common in applied economics and finance.

## Data Sources and Sample Construction {#sec-data-sources}

The panel is balanced and contains no missing values. In a real
application this subsection would list each external data source,
the time window covered, and the merge and filter rules applied
to arrive at the analysis sample.

## Variable Definitions {#sec-data-vars}

The outcome variable $y_{it}$ takes positive real values. The
focal regressor $x_{it}$ is continuous and roughly symmetric.
Three control variables $z^{1}_{it}, z^{2}_{it}, z^{3}_{it}$
capture observable unit characteristics. Full definitions are in
Appendix @sec-app-vardesc.

## Summary Statistics {#sec-data-summary}

Table @tbl-summary reports means and standard deviations for
each variable.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the example panel."
#| echo: false
#| eval: false
# knitr::kable(your_data_frame, booktabs = TRUE)
```

# Methodology {#sec-method}

Following @example2025, our baseline specification is a two-way
fixed-effects regression:

$$
y_{it} = \alpha_i + \delta_t + \beta\, x_{it} + \gamma' \mathbf{Z}_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}
$$ {#eq-baseline}

where $\alpha_i$ absorbs time-invariant unit heterogeneity,
$\delta_t$ absorbs common time shocks, and $\mathbf{Z}_{it}$ is
the vector of controls defined above. We cluster standard errors
at the unit level to allow for arbitrary within-unit serial
correlation.

# Main Results {#sec-results}

This section reports the baseline estimates and explores how they
vary across observable subgroups.

## Baseline Estimates {#sec-results-baseline}

Estimating @eq-baseline on the full sample yields a point estimate
of $\hat{\beta} = 0.12$ (standard error 0.03), statistically
significant at the 1% level.

## Heterogeneity {#sec-results-hetero}

Splitting the sample by tercile of a pre-period unit characteristic,
the estimate ranges from 0.04 in the bottom tercile to 0.21 in the
top tercile. The pattern is monotone and statistically distinct
across terciles.

## Mechanisms {#sec-results-mechanism}

Decomposing the headline estimate into its within-period and
across-period components shows that roughly two-thirds of the
effect operates contemporaneously, with the remainder unfolding
over the following two periods.

# Additional Analyses {#sec-additional}

A natural extension is to allow $\beta$ to vary with the macro
state. Letting $S_t \in \{0, 1\}$ indicate recession periods and
interacting it with $x_{it}$ yields an additional $0.05$ uplift
on the focal coefficient in recessions.

# Robustness {#sec-robust}

The baseline survives the usual battery of robustness checks:
dropping the most extreme 1% of observations, restricting the
panel to a balanced subsample, replacing fixed effects with
unit-specific linear trends, and re-clustering at alternative
levels. Detailed results appear in the Internet Appendix.

# Conclusion {#sec-conc}

This template paper has illustrated the typographic conventions
of the qkit paper format. Replace each section's contents with
your own analysis.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

```{=tex}
\newpage
\appendix
\setcounter{table}{0}
\setcounter{figure}{0}
\renewcommand{\thesection}{Appendix \Alph{section}}
\renewcommand{\thetable}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{table}}
\renewcommand{\thefigure}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{figure}}
\renewcommand{\theequation}{\Alph{section}.\arabic{equation}}
```

# Variable Definitions {#sec-app-vardesc}

This appendix would list formal definitions and data sources for
every variable used in @sec-data. Replace with your own table of
variable definitions.

# Auxiliary Derivations {#sec-app-derivations}

Selected derivations sketched in the main text.

::: {.proof}
A short proof of the proposition in @sec-theory-predictions:
under the stated assumption, within-unit demeaning eliminates
$\alpha_i$, leaving an estimating equation that satisfies the
standard OLS exogeneity condition. $\square$
:::
